summary: When I was young in Japan, my family was well-to-do and had tutors come to teach me samisen. It was part of my early education before I came to America. Although I did not pursue it as a profession, learning samisen was a reflection of my family's cultured background.

I landed in Seattle in 1913 but soon came to Fresno by train where my father and uncle met me. My father farmed about five miles north of Fresno, growing grapes. Fresno was hot and had malaria cases when I arrived, but over time the land developed and became cooler. I lived and farmed in Fresno County for many years, managing grape ranches and raising my family there.

I first landed at Seattle in 1913 after passing a physical exam there, which was less strict than San Francisco. The ship I took was from the Nihon Yusen Company, about 5,000 tons, smaller than ships going to San Francisco. Seattle impressed me with its streets made of Oregon pines and the vast tall trees I saw in Oregon on the train ride from there.

I was born and raised in Japan, specifically in Kochi Prefecture. My family was well-off, involved in the lumber business, and I learned samisen as a child. I returned to Japan around 1925 to take a wife, whom my father helped arrange. Japan was also where I went for medical treatment to cure hookworms before coming to America.

When I was preparing to come to America, I went through the Immigration Office in Kobe. They found I had hookworms there, which delayed my journey. I had to seek medical treatment in Hiroshima and other places before I could pass the physical exam and continue my trip.

After the Immigration Office in Kobe found I had hookworms, I went to doctors in Hiroshima and other places to get treatment. It took a long time to cure the hookworms before I could continue my journey to America. Hiroshima was part of the medical stops on my way to Seattle.

The ship I took from Japan to Seattle was operated by the Nihon Yusen Company. Their ships were about 5,000 tons, which was large for Japan at the time but smaller than the 12,000-ton ships going to San Francisco. The journey was long, and the ship carried about 300 passengers including many picture brides and men returning to Japan to find wives.

During the ship journey from Vancouver to Seattle, we had a long time on New Year's Day. The trip was slow and gave us time to get to know other passengers, including picture brides. Vancouver was a notable stop or point on the route before arriving in Seattle.

On the train ride from Seattle, I saw many tall Oregon pine trees standing over 200 feet tall in rows for miles. It was an impressive sight that I had never seen in Japan. Oregon's forests and lumber resources were remarkable and left a strong impression on me.

The Sacramento Bee published articles portraying Japanese as diligent but also as a threat to white society, which fueled anti-Japanese sentiment. MacLarty, who wrote anti-Japanese pieces, ironically employed Japanese gardeners and cooks himself. These stories contributed to the start of Japanese exclusion by shaping public opinion.

The Japanese exclusion began largely because of negative articles in newspapers like the Sacramento Bee, which portrayed Japanese people as a threat. This created fear and contempt among white Americans. Despite good relationships in neighborhoods, the media's portrayal led to exclusion policies that affected us deeply.

My uncle was a lieutenant who fought in the Russo-Japanese War. His military background made him strict and without much consideration when I worked pruning grape branches under his direction. The war was part of his past and influenced his character and how he managed work on the ranch.

California was where my family settled and farmed grapes starting about 70 years ago. When I arrived in 1913, land laws allowed Japanese immigrants to buy land, and my father brought money from Japan to purchase 100 acres. The state had its challenges, including anti-Japanese sentiment and the Great Depression, but it was home for us.

My uncle's wife was from Kochi Prefecture and was well-educated, having graduated from the girls' higher normal school. She taught at a girls' high school in Kochi before coming to America. Kochi Prefecture was part of our family's roots in Japan.

My younger brother attended high school in Caruthers and was the first graduate of that school. Afterward, he bought land and moved to Parlier. Caruthers was part of our family's life in Central California during the early 20th century.

My uncle moved to Parlier after some years, and my brother also bought land there. Parlier became a new home base for parts of our family farming operations in Central California. It was a nearby community where we expanded our agricultural activities.

The Great Depression hit us hard in Central California around 1929 when 29 banks in four counties went bankrupt. During that time, my wife died, and I struggled to manage 160 acres alone, eventually mortgaging the land. It was a difficult period, but I managed to buy back half the land later and continued farming.

Our farming operations were in Central California, including Fresno, Madera, Kings, and Tulare counties. This region was where we grew grapes and experienced the effects of the Great Depression and Japanese exclusion. It was home for our family and where we built our lives.

My father and uncle owned 100 acres in Fresno County, which they bought with money brought from Japan. I helped manage the ranch there after I arrived. Fresno County was the center of our agricultural work and family life for many years.

Madera County was one of the four Central California counties affected by the Great Depression bank failures. Though not directly mentioned as our land, it was part of the regional community where many Japanese farmers lived and worked alongside us.

Kings County was included in the four counties in Central California where 29 banks went bankrupt during the Great Depression. This event impacted the Japanese farming community, including families like mine who farmed nearby.

Tulare County was one of the counties in Central California where the banking crisis during the Great Depression affected many farmers. Our family was part of this broader agricultural region, facing economic hardships during that time.

During World War II, my two sons went to Europe. My oldest son went to Germany right before the war ended and stayed there as part of the occupation force. Meanwhile, I lived alone for 33 years after my wife died, managing the farm and raising my children during this difficult period.

My oldest son went to Germany near the end of World War II and stayed there as a member of the occupation force. This was part of his service during and after the war. Germany was far from home but became a place where my son contributed to post-war efforts.

After World War II, my oldest son remained in Germany as part of the occupation force. This was a significant period as the Allies administered post-war Germany. His role there was part of the broader efforts to rebuild and stabilize the country.

During the Great Depression, 29 banks in Central California counties went bankrupt, but Bank of America was not specifically mentioned in my story. However, the banking crisis affected many farmers like me who had to mortgage land to survive. The financial institutions' failures shaped our economic struggles in those years.

Growing grapes was the best kind of farming work because we could sell our grapes to Sun-Maid Growers of California. This gave us a reliable market and less worry about selling our crop. Over time, most of the work became mechanized except for watering, making grape farming more manageable.
